Unfortunately, their aspirations have met a harsh reality. Approximately 600 men find themselves confined in a makeshift prison, hindered from escape by armed guards. The conditions in this warehouse are becoming increasingly dire, as authorities have ceased the distribution of food and essential supplies. "I traveled from Djibouti seeking work. Previously, they provided us with minimal food. Without sustenance, survival is in jeopardy," lamented one migrant, choosing to remain anonymous. "If no resolution emerges, we face the likelihood of deportation back to our countries or worse," he added. Yemen, often viewed as a transit hub for those escaping hunger and strife in East Africa, has become synonymous with danger for many who undertake this journey. The treacherous route, which was perilous even before Yemen's 2015 conflict, sees hundreds lose their lives annually at sea, while survivors contend with threats of violence, exploitation, and the inherent risks of war, as reported by the UNHCR. Despite these grave dangers, the influx of migrants crossing the Gulf of Aden and Red Sea continues to rise, reaching 117,000 in 2016 compared to 92,000 the previous year, according to the UNHCR. Local official Khaled al-Elwany, who once managed the deportation of these migrants, estimates that up to 15,000 are apprehended at city checkpoints monthly. He asserts he lost his job for resisting plans to relocate the detention center and for raising concerns over the halted food provisions. A ministry representative told Reuters that relocating the facility aimed to enhance conditions for migrants awaiting deportation. As Saudi Arabia and its Gulf allies engage in efforts to restore Yemen’s displaced government, the governing body struggles with internal conflicts, leaving state services and salaries severely disrupted. The African migrants, facing neglect, are seemingly at the lowest end of the priority list. The anxious men confined in the Aden warehouse, relying on scant funds for bread and washing their clothes in a filthy outdoor area, reflect only a fraction of the broader crisis.
